title: Delight in God's People
speaker: Elder Andrew Hoy
source_files: 6-30-24 -AM- Delight in God's People - 1 Thessalonians 1 - Elder Andrew Hoy.txt

outline.title: Introduction: A Bittersweet Morning
subpoints: Elder Hoy's final sermon as an elder at this church
Personal story of finding and joining the church 9.5 years ago
Theme: delight in God's people leads to sacrificial service

outline.title: The Origin of Paul's Delight: The Backstory of the Thessalonian Church
subpoints: Paul's second missionary journey — passing through Macedonia to Thessalonica
Acts 17:1-5: Paul went to the synagogue; Jews were largely unpersuaded but many devout Greeks believed
A mob was stirred up, Jason was dragged before the rulers
Paul and Silas were not fleeing — they were sent by the church
The church's uncertain beginning: young, without a strong leader left behind, amid hostility

outline.title: The Expression of Paul's Delight
subpoints: Paul gives thanks always, constantly (1 Thess. 1:2) — superlative, over-the-top language
Repeated in 2:13 — we also thank God constantly
In 2:17 — we endeavored more eagerly to see your face with great desire
In 2:20 — you are our glory and joy
In 2:7 — Paul compares himself to a nursing mother who cherishes her own children
In 2:11 — a father with his own children
Affectionately longing (2:8), laboring night and day (2:9)

outline.title: The Reasons for Paul's Delight
subpoints: At first glance: they received the gospel gladly (1 Thess. 1:3)
But the foundational reason is in vv. 4-5: their election by God — the gospel came in power and in the Holy Spirit
Their obedience and perseverance bore testimony to the sovereign work of God
Their faithful witness had spread throughout all Macedonia and Achaia (1:8)
They turned from idols to serve the living and true God — a remarkable testimony in the dark pagan world
Two lessons: we connect best with those who share our deepest thing — the same Lord; and our delight should have spiritual underpinnings, not just temporal commonalities

outline.title: The Result of Paul's Delight: Sacrificial Service
subpoints: 1 Thessalonians 2:17-3:5: Paul couldn't come back himself, so he sent Timothy
Delight in people naturally produces pouring yourself out for them
Parents with children; the difference between serving from duty vs. from joy
Application: whether you feel it or not, serve; but God's design is for delight in the saints to make service a joy not a burden

summary: Elder Andrew Hoy delivers what he describes as likely his final sermon as an elder at Bible Presbyterian Church of Olympia. Taking 1 Thessalonians 1 as his text, he argues that delight in God's people leads to sacrificial service. He opens with a warm personal reflection on finding and joining the congregation nine and a half years earlier, then traces the backstory of the Thessalonian church from Acts 17 — a turbulent, short-lived church plant that nonetheless became one of the most encouraging to the Apostle Paul. Hoy shows that Paul's delight in the Thessalonians was not superficial — it overflowed in superlatives of thanksgiving, maternal and paternal imagery, and the sending of Timothy at personal cost. He argues that the foundation of Paul's delight was not merely their responsiveness, but the sovereign work of God in their election and the powerful coming of the gospel — their obedience was testimony to that work. The sermon closes with a call for the congregation to examine whether they delight in God's people with the same spiritual foundation, and to let that delight overflow in sacrificial service to one another.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading and Prayer

1 Thessalonians chapter 1. This is the Word of God.

Paul, Silvanus, and Timothy, to the Church of the Thessalonians in God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ. We give thanks to God always for you, making mention of you in our prayers, remembering without ceasing your work of faith, labor of love and patience of hope in our Lord Jesus Christ, in the sight of our God and Father, knowing, beloved brethren, your election by God. For our gospel did not come to you in word only, but also in power and in the Holy Spirit and in much assurance, as you know what kind of men we were among you for your sake. And you became followers of us and of the Lord having received the word in much affliction with joy of the Holy Spirit, so that you became examples to all in Macedonia and Achaia who believe. For from you the word of the Lord has sounded forth, not only in Macedonia and in Achaia, but also in every place. Your faith toward God has gone out so that we do not need to say anything. For they themselves declare concerning us what manner of entry we had to you and how you turned to God from idols to serve the living and true God and to wait for his Son from heaven, whom he raised from the dead, even Jesus who delivers us from the wrath to come.

This is the Word of God. Let's pray. Gracious God, we thank you for your Word. Pray that you would be with Elder Hoy now as he brings the truth to us. We ask that you would bless his preaching in our hearing. We ask this in Christ's name, amen.

## Introduction

Welcome, Bible Presbyterian Church of Olympia — this is a great honor and privilege to bring God's Word to you all this morning. This is likely the last time I will stand before you as an Elder of this Church, and so this is a particularly bittersweet morning for me. But as I come into this final sermon and kind of teaching as Elder in this Church, my mind has gone back to nine and a half years ago, when Katie and I had to make the difficult decision to leave our previous church.

That had been a particularly difficult decision for multiple reasons. It was the church where Katie and I met one another. It was a church where I had been discipled in a pivotal season of my life. It was also a church where we attended when our first three children were born, and it was a church where we really had deep roots and loving friendships. However, because of my growing biblical and theological convictions, which with time were departing more and more from those of the elders at the church we had attended, we finally came to the place where we decided it would be most wise for us to find a home that was more in alignment with what we believe the Scriptures taught.

So, again — that was nine and a half years ago. I made a list of 15 churches, not that I thought we would land at all of them, but I figured it was a big decision and we wanted to make sure we weren't missing one. And as we worked our way through that list, the second church we visited was a small Presbyterian church on Slater Kenny that I knew virtually nothing about. I drove past it probably hundreds of times — I used to deliver pizza in Lacey, so Slater Kenny was a main thoroughfare. And really the only reason it stood out in my mind was because once, while I was delivering pizza, there was this strange scene where there was a car caught over the berm into the ditch going out the front. You can ask me during lunch if you're curious what that was about.

Theologically I actually knew very little about the Bible Presbyterians as well. I heard rumors that they were dispensational and fundamentalists. But we did have some friends who attended here — we knew the Lutzes — so we decided to check it out. I still remember that first Sunday very vividly, awkwardly coming in, not sure which door was the entrance, not sure which pews might be reserved. But we made our way back — I think we sat around where the Cleavies are sitting over there — and shortly after sitting down, this Brazilian man came over to us and introduced himself, and learned all of our names. Worship was about to begin. He got up here and proceeded to introduce my entire family, having memorized each and every one of our names.

Now there were only five of us at that point in time, not eight, so it was a little bit easier of a task. But yeah, it made quite the impression. I don't remember if we stayed for potluck that first Sunday. I think we did — probably went first in line as well.

That list of 15 churches I very quickly narrowed down to two. We did, after visiting here, visit one other church, which could have been the one we ended up at — theologically there was a lot of alignment. But a few things stood out to us about this church: they had their own building, which was nice, but even more so — the singing. And add to that the impression that Pastor T2 had made on our first Sunday. A couple of weeks later we decided that this was going to be our new home. As I look back on the decisions I've had to make over the last 10 or 20 years of my life, the decision to land here I can confidently say is one of the best that I have made for myself and my family.

Well, people of the church, my temptation was to just get up here and say lots of wonderful things about you all this morning, to share fond memories and to express how grateful me and my family are for you all. But I don't think that would be quite appropriate — that is not what this time is for. This time is to hear the word of God proclaimed and expounded for your edification and your strength. But in keeping with my desires, I have chosen a text in which we see the delight of the people of God — particularly in which we see the Apostle Paul's delight in a particular people of God, that is in the people of the Thessalonian church.

As I read through the New Testament letters, this is a church that I've often found much in common with our own church here. And I've often been related to the Apostle Paul's great love and desire for that church as I've read about it.

So our morning text is 1 Thessalonians chapter 1. And one of the things we see there is that delight in God's people leads to sacrificial service. We will look at this in four parts: the origin of the Apostle Paul's delight in the Thessalonian church, the expression of Paul's delight, the reasons for his delight, and the result of it.

## The Origin of Paul's Delight

So to begin — the origin of Paul's delight in the Thessalonian church. This is a church that, if you know anything about church planting, it did not start in the ideal way. It was not a model church plant. Typically at least in our times, when someone wants to plant a church, this is something that takes months and even years of preparation. Oftentimes it will begin with a man who has a burden and calling to see a church started in an area, and that man will go through training and then eventually try to establish a small group in a town or a location. Eventually that small group can become a mission work, and with time it can be birthed into a church.

This is not the story of the Thessalonian church. The Apostle Paul — he is on what has been called his second missionary journey, having already gone around the Mediterranean area once proclaiming the word of the gospel and establishing churches. From Antioch he went up through Asia Minor, what we would call Turkey today, through Thrace and down toward Greece. And to get down toward Greece he had to pass through Macedonia, and in that process he goes to the town of Thessalonica.

If you would all turn with me to Acts 17, we read the account of the Apostle Paul as he arrives in Thessalonica.

Acts 17, beginning at verse 1: Now when they had passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where there was a synagogue of the Jews. Then Paul, as his custom was, went in to them, and for three Sabbaths reasoned with them from the Scriptures, explaining and demonstrating that the Christ had to suffer and rise again from the dead, and saying, 'This Jesus whom I preach to you is the Christ.' And some of them were persuaded, and a great multitude of the devout Greeks and not a few of the leading women joined Paul and Silas.

So we see here the beginning of this work began with Paul doing what he would normally do — going to the Jews first and then to Gentiles. He goes to the synagogue and begins to try to persuade them that Jesus was the long-anticipated Messiah. And in this synagogue there were faithful devout Jews but there were also Gentiles — what someone called God-fearers — Gentiles who had been compelled by the faith of the Jews but who were not ethnically Jewish themselves. And as Paul is proclaiming the truth of the gospel, it's actually not very many of the Jews who are persuaded — it's actually more of the Gentiles.

But at verse 5: the Jews who were not persuaded, becoming envious, took some of the evil men from the marketplace and, gathering a mob, set all the city in an uproar and attacked the house of Jason and sought to bring them out to the people. But when they did not find them, they dragged Jason and some brethren to the rulers of the city, crying out, 'These who have turned the world upside down have come here too. Jason has harbored them, and these are all acting contrary to the decrees of Caesar, saying there is another King — Jesus.' And they troubled the crowd and the rulers of the city when they heard these things. So when they had taken security from Jason and the rest, they let them go. Then the brethren immediately sent Paul and Silas away by night to Berea.

So we see that though the gospel message did not take root among the Jews, those who were particularly disturbed by Paul contracted some roughnecks from down the street to stir up a riot. This led to accusations — Paul and Silas were accused of turning the world upside down, which was true. And yet the chaos was so great that Paul and Silas had no option but to leave.

But I think there's an important word here. It doesn't just say that Paul and Silas left as if they were abandoning the people — it says they were sent. The impression we get is that it wasn't just Paul and Silas who looked around and said, this is getting really bad, let's get out of here and leave this young church that we've only spent about a month with. Rather, the whole church with Paul and Silas — they're praying together — and they all mutually decide that it is going to be best for the Apostle Paul and Silas to move on. Because the Apostle Paul, as we will see later, has such a great love for this church, this certainly could not have been a decision that he made lightly.

So this is some of the backstory from the book of Acts. If this was all we had about the Thessalonian church, I think we'd all be wondering how it would go. As far as we know, there's no talk of a strong leader in their midst who would carry on the baton as Paul and Silas leave. One of the chances of this young church plant surviving such a short period with the Apostle Paul — it's not like Paul left on peaceful terms.

But praise be to God, we do get to hear more of what happens. As we move forward to the book of 1 Thessalonians, we get to see that they did persevere, they did last, and that Paul was able to maintain his connection and relationship with them.

## The Expression of Paul's Delight

So, following along in 1 Thessalonians — the expression of Paul's delight. What continues as the story goes on is that there are really three things the Apostle Paul particularly mentions about them: they received the gospel in power and in truth (1:5); they persevered and endured and were faithful through much affliction; and they witnessed — even as a band of young and immature believers in pagan territory, their witness had spread throughout the region. In verse 8 of chapter 1, we read: for from you the word of the Lord has sounded forth, not only in Macedonia and in Achaia, but also in every place. Your faith toward God has gone out so that we do not need to say anything.

They had turned away from their idols — they had turned away from the wickedness of their pagan culture. And at that point in time this would have been quite a strange thing to do, knowing that it would likely bring more persecution on them. But as more persecution came and they persevered through it, what a glorious testimony to the truth of what they were holding on to.

Now, this was not a church without challenges. They were young and vulnerable. There were certain questions beginning to surface. One was questioning whether the Apostle Paul was really genuine in his ministry. And there were some challenges with a certain spiritual lethargy — Paul is saying, you've done well, but don't stop, continue to grow in your faith more and more, continue in your sanctification, continue in your growth in godliness, don't settle.

This honestly is why I've often thought of this church when reading about the Thessalonians. They don't have a lot of the great heresy of the Galatian church or the rampant over-sinfulness of the Corinthian church. Largely a good group of people who love one another — but who need to be encouraged to continue in that love and not settle by looking around and saying, hey, we're doing pretty good compared to everyone else. But where are we? Are we growing? That's at least a word any Christian in a comfortable place should be asking themselves.

Now, while this was an impressive young church who had had a great witness in the area, they are not a church without challenges. And as we consider this before moving on to talk about the Apostle Paul's actual delight in this people, I just want to note the importance of reading the Bible with a sense of context and audience.

So let me say something about Paul's expression of his delight. The Apostle Paul begins as a letter with a fairly standard greeting, but then jumping right in at verse 2, he begins with we give thanks — and he gives us a couple superlatives that add to this thanks: we give thanks always, constantly mentioning you in our prayers. So it wasn't enough to just say we give thanks always, it wasn't enough to just say we give thanks constantly — but we give thanks always, constantly. And this is the kind of over-the-top language the Apostle Paul continues to use about his gratitude for this people.

We see it repeated in chapter 2 verse 13: we also thank God constantly for his work among you. In 2:17: we endeavored more eagerly and with great desire to see you face to face. And then in 2:20: you are our glory and joy.

But the Apostle Paul doesn't just express his joy in words — he also expresses his personal love through various pictures. In chapter 2 verse 7 he compares himself to a nursing mother who cherishes her own children. In 2:11 he compares himself to a father with his own children. In 2:8 he says, affectionately longing for you, we were pleased to impart to you our own lives. And in 2:9, describing the labor he had put forth in his care, worry, and prayer toward them, he says: we labor in toil night and day.

The Apostle Paul dearly loved this church. And as we read this, I hope that we find ourselves asking, how about me? What impression do I give about those who God in his sovereign hand has pulled me together with into sweet fellowship?

Do you have frequent and regular expressions of your delight in others? I think we could probably fall into about three groups in the church. There are those who genuinely delight in the fellowship of the saints and regularly express it. There are also others who genuinely love one another — genuinely love the fellowship, genuinely love the church — but aren't so good at expressing it. To you I challenge you: express it more. It's something that some do much better than others, but remember it is encouraging to come to a church where you know that you're loved.

And yet I think there has to be another group of people. I don't know who you are, because like I said not everybody expresses it. And I hope this group doesn't exist, but there are those who maybe don't delight in the people of God, who don't delight in the fellowship of the saints, who don't delight in this current body. If that's you — repent. But I also want to say you're missing out. If you fly out of here as soon as the service is over maybe a couple minutes over so you don't have to greet someone else, you're missing out.

## The Reasons for Paul's Delight

So Paul takes great joy and delight in the saints and in this sweet little church who has persevered through so much. But Paul makes clear on several occasions throughout this letter what his reasons are for delighting in this church.

If we begin at 1:3, we might at first get a wrong impression that it is merely because they were a church who received with gladness what he had to bring. And 1:3 we read: we give thanks to God always for you all, making mention of you in prayers, remembering without ceasing your work of faith, labor of love, and patience of hope in our Lord Jesus Christ. So it is true that he takes delight in them because of the way they received the word and responded.

But if we stop there and didn't actually keep on reading, I don't think we're getting the real foundation for his delight. Because the Apostle Paul would have other churches who are maybe less enthusiastic and responsive, and he still delights in them. I think the real key is actually in verses 4 and 5: knowing, beloved brethren, your election by God. For our gospel did not come to you in word only, but also in power and in the Holy Spirit and in much assurance.

I see verses 4 and 5 as foundational because there he talks about God — the sovereign one who worked in their lives to save them. Their joyful response, their active faith, their perseverance — all of that is an overflow, a testimony to the work that God did. It wasn't just, hey, this is a really obedient group of people, I like them. It was because of the witness that their obedience bore to the faithfulness of God that created delight.

So at his foundation, what was Paul's reason for delight? It was the work of God in their lives — which then manifested itself in their glad reception of the word and their faithful testimony.

In chapter 2 verse 13 he says this very clearly: for this reason we also thank God without ceasing, because when you received the word of God which you heard from us, you welcomed it not as the word of men, but as it is in truth, the word of God, which also effectively works in you who believe. So there we see it — it is the God who is working, and them who are acting.

And there is also their faithful testimony — how they had a testimony that spread to the surrounding region, and what it consisted of: turning from idols. I teach at a classical Christian school, and last year we read a whole bunch of pagan literature — Gilgamesh, the Odyssey, the works of Herodotus, the plays of Sophocles. And one of the advantages of having read these great works of the past is that you see the bleak outlook of the pagan world. You have a much better understanding of how dark things really were before Christ. When you understand how dark it is, you begin to better understand why this testimony of the work of God at this church was so radiant, how it spread. This was not something you saw in the ancient world — people just turning away from their idols and finding radically changed lives. At least causing people to ask themselves, what is going on?

Two points of application: First, we all know what it's like to connect with someone with whom we have much in common. But if you're the person who does not really delight in the fellowship of the saints — if you truly don't delight in this fellowship — I'd ask you to remember what you have in common with the believers. The truth is you might have nothing else in common with them. You might like different music, different movies, different people. But all of those things pale in comparison to what you do have in common. You serve the same God who saved you and saved them. You are being sanctified by the same God. You are bearing witness to the same God in this world. You have the same hope and the same eternity — and it is these people you will be spending eternity with.

Second, for those of you who say, yeah, I love being here, I love hanging out with the people — I'd ask you to question yourself: what is the reason for your delight in the people? Is it just that this is a cool place and you've found a lot of new friends who like all the same things you like? Make sure your delight in God's people has spiritual underpinnings. Delight in anybody who would call Jesus Lord, because that is the greatest point of commonality.

## The Result: Sacrificial Service

Finally, what is the result of the Apostle Paul's delight in the Thessalonian church? Simply put: sacrificial service.

1 Thessalonians 2:17-3:5, we read: but we, brethren, having been taken away from you for a short time in presence, not in heart, endeavored more eagerly to see your face with great desire. Therefore we wanted to come to you, even I, Paul, time and again, but Satan hindered us. For what is our hope, or joy, or crown of rejoicing? Is it not even you in the presence of our Lord Jesus Christ at his coming? For you are our glory and joy. Therefore, when we could no longer endure it, we thought it good to be left in Athens alone and sent Timothy, our brother and minister of God and our fellow laborer in the gospel of Christ, to establish you and encourage you concerning your faith.

The Apostle Paul didn't actually have an opportunity to go back to be with them, to actually pour himself out in their presence. So how did he pour himself out? In prayer and giving and writing this letter to them. But also in sending Timothy — his beloved co-laborer — though I'm sure it was probably tempting to want to hold on to Timothy to keep him laboring alongside him. In love for these saints, he sends Timothy out.

This last point of application is a no-brainer for any of us, because we all understand that if we truly delight in something, we naturally pour ourselves out into it. If we truly love something, we give ourselves to it. Think of a man with his car, parents with their children — why do parents do what they do, getting up at night with little babies, changing diapers, gazing over them with gratitude? Because they love them. Those things that we love, those things that we delight in, we give ourselves to.

So in a sense, if you attend to everything else in this sermon, this point takes care of itself. If you truly delight in God's people, you will pour yourself out in sacrificial service. Now, does this mean you don't need to serve sacrificially if you don't feel it? No — you're accountable to God's commands regardless. But consider this: God's design for us is to delight in those things he calls us to do. And when we truly delight in the people of God, what could otherwise become burdensome or tiresome can become a delight in itself because of the object of our service.

Furthermore, when you are the recipient of someone else's service, what do you appreciate more — when you have the sense that someone is serving you out of joy and delight, or when someone serves you out of a sense of duty? Consider your grandparents — I had grandparents who absolutely loved me and poured themselves into me, and it was evident that any sacrifice they made was not even a sacrifice at all.

So yes, whether you feel it or not, you should still be pouring yourself out in service to the people of God. But you will be a greater blessing to those you serve when it truly comes from a place of love, joy, and delight in them.

People of the Bible Presbyterian Church of Olympia, when I think of this church, I can truly say that you are a delight. It has been a joy, honor, and privilege to serve you for six years. I've done so imperfectly. I am still growing into the image of my Savior, the great servant of all, each and every day. Jim, I cry too. But people, my hope and prayer for you is that as the Apostle Paul loved and encouraged the Thessalonian Church to more and more grow into the image of their Savior and into the holiness and their delight for one another — so would be the case for each and every one of you.

Let's pray. Gracious Almighty God, we thank you that you are a saving God. We thank you for the great record that we have of your saving works throughout history. We thank you that in your sovereign perfect will you have decided to save sinners. Father, we thank you for the work in the Thessalonian Church and the glorious testimony that they had in their time. Father, I pray that here in Olympia you would continue your good and saving work in this time. Father, I pray that you would knit this people, this church here in Lacey, Olympia, Washington, that you would continue to grow them in their love for one another, and that as you showed your love to them and they grew in their love for one another, that they would be a people knit together. And in their testimony in this surrounding area, Father, we know that we need your Spirit to do these things. So may your Spirit strengthen us this day. We pray this in Christ's precious name. Amen.
